text: Joab son of Zeruiah realized that the king's heart was turning toward Absalom.

So Joab sent to Tekoa and brought back a wise woman from there. He told her, "Pretend to be in mourning — put on mourning clothes, don't use any oil, and act like someone who has been grieving a death for a long time."
"Then go to the king and say this to him," and Joab told her exactly what to say.
When the woman from Tekoa came before the king, she fell face-down on the ground in homage and said, "Help me, my king!"
The king asked her, "What's wrong?" She said, "I am indeed a widow — my husband has died."
"Your servant had two sons. Out in the field, with no one around to separate them, they got into a fight, and one struck the other down and killed him."

"Now the whole family has turned against your servant, saying, 'Hand over the one who killed his brother, so we can put him to death for his brother's life' — even though that would destroy the heir too. They would put out my last remaining coal and leave my husband with no name or descendant on earth."
The king told the woman, "Go home, and I'll issue an order on your behalf."
The woman from Tekoa said to the king, "My lord the king, let the guilt fall on me and my father's house — let the king and his throne be innocent of it."
The king said, "If anyone speaks against you, bring him to me, and he will never trouble you again."

Then she said, "Please, may the king invoke the LORD your God, so the avenger of blood won't cause any more destruction and my son won't be killed." He replied, "As surely as the LORD lives, not a single hair of your son will fall to the ground."
Then the woman said, "Please, let your servant say one more thing to my lord the king." He said, "Speak."
She said, "Then why have you devised something similar against the people of God? By making this decision, doesn't the king convict himself, since he hasn't brought his own banished son home?"

"We're all going to die — we're like water poured out on the ground, which can't be gathered up again. But God doesn't just take away life; he works out ways so the banished one isn't kept cut off from him forever."
"Now, the reason I've come to say this to my lord the king is that people have made me afraid, and your servant thought, 'I'll speak to the king — maybe he'll grant his servant's request.'"
"The king will surely listen and rescue his servant from the hand of the man trying to cut off both me and my son from the inheritance God gave us."
"Your servant thought, 'Let the word of my lord the king bring me relief,' since my lord the king is able to judge good and evil like an angel of God. May the LORD your God be with you!"
The king answered the woman, "Don't hold anything back from me that I ask you." She said, "Let my lord the king speak."

The king asked, "Is Joab's hand behind all of this?" The woman answered, "As surely as you live, my king, no one can turn right or left from anything my lord the king says. Yes — it was your servant Joab who gave the orders and put all these words in your servant's mouth."
"Your servant Joab did this to turn the situation around. But my lord has wisdom like an angel of God, knowing everything that happens in the land."
Then the king said to Joab, "Very well, I grant it — go and bring back the young man Absalom."
Joab fell face-down to the ground in homage and blessed the king. He said, "Today your servant knows he has found favor with you, my king, because the king has granted his servant's request."
So Joab got up, went to Geshur, and brought Absalom back to Jerusalem.
But the king said, "Let him go to his own house — he is not to come into my presence." So Absalom went to his own house and did not appear before the king.
Now in all Israel there was no one praised for his handsome looks the way Absalom was — from the sole of his foot to the top of his head, he had no flaw at all.
Whenever he cut the hair on his head — he cut it once a year, since it grew too heavy for him — he would weigh it, and it came to two hundred shekels by the royal standard.
Absalom had three sons and one daughter, whose name was Tamar; she was a beautiful woman.
Absalom lived in Jerusalem for two full years without ever coming before the king.
Then Absalom sent for Joab to send him to the king, but Joab would not come to him. He sent for him a second time, but Joab still refused to come.
So Absalom told his servants, "Look, Joab's field is right next to mine, and he has barley growing there — go set it on fire." So Absalom's servants set the field ablaze.
Then Joab got up, went to Absalom's house, and demanded, "Why did your servants set my field on fire?"

Absalom answered Joab, "Look, I sent word telling you to come here so I could send you to the king to ask, 'Why did I even bother coming back from Geshur? I'd have been better off staying there.' Now let me see the king's face, and if I'm guilty of anything, let him put me to death."
So Joab went to the king and told him everything. The king then called for Absalom, who came in and bowed down with his face to the ground before the king — and the king kissed Absalom.
